bibliographic.title: Purity, a Middle English Poem

entities.label: Robert J. Menner edition, Purity, a Middle English Poem (1920)
notes: Menner selected Purity as his title instead of Cleanness or Clannesse.
The edition was prepared from manuscript rotographs or photographs and checked against the manuscript's offsets, corrections, and earlier editions.
Introduction pp. vii–lxii; edited text pp. 1–66; notes pp. 67–178; glossary pp. 179–214; bibliography pp. 215–219; appendix pp. 221–234.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1920, and both the current U-M item and Internet Archive record present the digitized material as public domain in the United States.
rights.component: 1920 printed edition, including editorial text and apparatus

entities.description: The unique manuscript witness of Cleanness and also of Pearl, Patience, and Sir Gawain and the Green Knight. The Cleanness catalog component occupies modern folios 60r–86r, with prefatory drawings on 60r–60v and text on 61r–86r; corresponding older ink foliation is 56r–82r and 57r–82r.

historical_designations: Cotton MS Nero A X, article 3
Cotton MS Nero A X, ff. 41–130
holding.notes: The original composite Cotton MS Nero A X was separated into two volumes in 1964.

entities.label: British Library Cotton MS Nero A X/2

physicalDescription.extent: 90 folios in the separately bound volume; Cleanness at modern folios 60r–86r
physicalDescription.format: Parchment codex, written in one column of 36 lines
notes: Gothic cursive script described as textura rotunda with Anglicana features.
One scribe copied the manuscript in a South-East Cheshire or North-East Staffordshire dialect; the poet's dialect is distinguished as farther south in Staffordshire.
Two drawings precede Cleanness and thirteen decorated initials occur within it; modern f. 86r contains the poem's final eleven lines and the first illustration for Patience.
Modern pencil foliation ff. 41–130 corresponds to older ink foliation ff. 37–126.

rights.basis: The text is an anonymous fourteenth-century work; this statement is confined to the medieval textual content and does not cover modern photographs, metadata, editions, or access contracts.
rights.component: Anonymous medieval textual content of Cleanness

rights.basis: Current imaging terms say supplied images must not be copied or reproduced without written permission and that permission or licensing fees may apply.
rights.component: Images supplied through British Library Imaging Services
notes: This contractual reuse condition is separate from the copyright status of the fourteenth-century text and from Commons's claim for its hosted derivative.

bibliographic.publisher: Humphrey Milford, Oxford University Press for the Early English Text Society
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society Original Series 162
bibliographic.title: Pearl, Cleanness, Patience and Sir Gawain, Reproduced in Facsimile from the Unique MS Cotton Nero A.x

entities.label: Gollancz facsimile of Cotton Nero A X/2 (1923)

physicalDescription.extent: 44 pages of introduction, two leaves, and photographic facsimile of old-numbered folios 37–126
notes: The facsimile covers the complete manuscript now separately bound as Cotton MS Nero A X/2.

rights.basis: The volume was published in 1923; Internet Archive metadata for the reviewed scan reports no visible copyright notice and not-in-copyright status.
rights.component: 1923 printed introduction and photographic facsimile
notes: This status concerns the 1923 publication in the United States; it does not determine the contractual terms for modern BL-commissioned images.

reviewNotes: Work-level title, author, composition, genre, subjects, and literary sources are cataloged in the companion metadata manifest; this file confines itself to textual and reproduction lineage, access, editorial method, and rights evidence.
The BL catalog's Cleanness range modern ff. 60r–86r includes two prefatory illustrations; text proper is modern ff. 61r–86r. Old ink ranges are ff. 56r–82r and 57r–82r respectively.
The complete Commons PDF and both Gollancz scans map the whole physical codex and therefore include structured work_portion locators rather than being mislabeled as work-only files.

lineage.summary: The repository XML is a local representation of University of Michigan CME item ACS0188.0001.001. U-M encoded the title page and 1,812-line Middle English poem from Robert J. Menner's 1920 edition Purity while retaining page milestones and images for the edition's largely untranscribed front and back matter. Menner edited the unique medieval witness, British Library Cotton MS Nero A X/2, with reference to manuscript photographs and Morris's earlier editions. Printed editions, scans of those editions, the U-M page-image package, the physical manuscript, manuscript facsimiles, and Olsen's later diplomatic edition are modeled separately. The complete downloadable manuscript surrogate is microfilm-derived; Commons and the University of Calgary present materially different rights claims, so neither claim is transferred to the other provider or to British Library imaging orders.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous (Pearl Poet)

catalogSummary.title: Cleanness

catalogingSubject.label: Cleanness
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The anonymous late-fourteenth-century Middle English alliterative poem conventionally titled Cleanness and also published as Purity, beginning ‘Clannesse who so kyndly cowþe comende.’ This subject excludes the other three poems in Cotton MS Nero A X/2, Menner's introduction, notes, glossary, bibliography, and appendix, the manuscript as a physical object, the University of Michigan encoding and page images, and the repository XML.

assertion.notes: Late-fourteenth-century composition is the conservative conventional dating used here. Menner's secure lower bound is dependence on the Old French Mandeville after about 1355–1356; the witness's copying range supplies attestation rather than a precise composition date.

notes: The normalized 1375–1399 range supports corpus discovery and sibling-work consistency; it is not an independently documented date of composition.
This is the production range of the unique surviving copy, distinct from composition and from the circa 1400–1410 date of its miniatures.

assertion.notes: Richard Morris printed the poem in the first edition of Early English Alliterative Poems in 1864; the cached scan is the explicitly revised second edition of 1869.

extent.description: One verse work. The repository XML encodes 1,812 L elements in 88 LG elements and twenty numbered or suffixed DIV1 sections (1–11, 11a, 12, 12a, 13, 13a–13c). Those groups and twenty supplied narrative headings reproduce Menner's editorial presentation and are not treated as an uncontested intrinsic stanza or section structure.

formStatements.description: A poem of 1,812 unrhymed alliterative long lines. The manuscript has grouping marks at four-line intervals, but Menner cautions that the work cannot securely be divided into four-line stanzas.

notes: The U-M and repository title Purity belongs to Menner's edition; Cleanness is used as the preferred work title without rewriting source-layer labels.

The manuscript's modern BL range for the catalog component is ff. 60r–86r, including two prefatory illustrations; the written poem begins on f. 61r. Older ink foliation is ff. 56r–82r for the component and ff. 57r–82r for the text.
Menner's New Haven and London publication places describe the 1920 edition and are not medieval composition places.

next_steps: Consult recent specialist bibliography on the Pearl Poet's chronology, dialect, and authorship.
Keep witness copying, dialect localization, and work composition explicitly separate.
openQuestions.question: Can current specialist scholarship narrow the poem's composition date or strengthen or revise the conventional common-authorship attribution for the four Cotton Nero A X/2 poems?

next_steps: Compare all thirteen decorated manuscript initials and four-line grouping marks with Menner's twenty headings and the 88 XML line groups.
Do not expose the XML's TYPE='stanza' label as an uncontested work-level stanza count.
openQuestions.question: Which large divisions and four-line grouping marks should be treated as intrinsic to the medieval poem rather than manuscript mise-en-page or later editorial presentation?

notes: England is the cultural and linguistic setting; no exact place of composition is established.

assertion.notes: The BL catalog distinguishes the poet's dialect, thought to be farther south in Staffordshire, from that of the surviving scribe.

notes: Dialect localization is not proof of an exact composition place or authorial residence.
This localizes the surviving copy's scribe, not necessarily the poet or place of composition.
placeStatements.placeLabel: South-East Cheshire or North-East Staffordshire, England

scope.description: Scribal language of the unique manuscript witness

assertion.notes: Pearl Poet, also called the Gawain Poet, is a conventional label for the unknown author associated with the four poems in Cotton MS Nero A X/2, not an attested personal name.

notes: Common authorship of Cleanness, Pearl, Patience, and Sir Gawain and the Green Knight is widely accepted from internal evidence but cannot be treated as a documented identity.

scope.description: Edited Middle English text on pp. 1–66, prepared from manuscript photographs and checked against the witness and prior editions

subjects.label: Belshazzar, Daniel, Jerusalem, and the sacred vessels

summaries.value: Cleanness is an anonymous Middle English alliterative poem that praises spiritual purity by contrasting it with defilement and divine punishment. Framed by the parable of the Wedding Feast and its garment, it develops three large biblical exempla—the Flood, the destruction of Sodom, and Belshazzar's profanation of Jerusalem's sacred vessels—before ending with an exhortation to confession, penance, and clean living.

The poem comprises 1,812 alliterative long lines. The manuscript marks four-line groups and has thirteen decorated major initials, while Menner supplies twenty descriptive section headings and retains Morris's line numbering by fours. The repository's twenty DIV1 elements and 88 LG elements therefore describe the source edition's representation, not a securely authorial stanza system.

Menner identifies the Vulgate Bible as the principal narrative source and documents use of the Old French Mandeville and Jean de Meun's Roman de la Rose. These literary sources and influences belong to intellectual history and are not physical-textual lineage entities in the companion manifest.

assertion.notes: The poem is untitled in its unique manuscript. Cleanness is the preferred modern title used by the British Library and the later diplomatic edition reviewed here.
Purity is Menner's 1920 editorial title and therefore the title displayed by the immediate source edition and U-M item.
Clannesse is sometimes used as a title from the opening word; it is not a manuscript title heading.

Framed by the parable of the Wedding Feast and its garment, it develops three large biblical exempla—the Flood, the destruction of Sodom, and Belshazzar's profanation of Jerusalem's sacred vessels—before ending with an exhortation to confession, penance, and clean living.
